content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Absurd really shocking dark comedies

**Score:** 63
**Body:** Looking for a pitch black comedy like Happiness to watch with a group of friends. Please no tame comedies, only the most insanely disturbing comedic stuff. I want to be like “wtf” the whole film. Favorite directors are Todd Solondz and David Lynch 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Swiss Army Man -- i still have no idea what the fuck i watched and it's been TEN YEARS
2. The Coffee Table - about a couple and their new baby. They invite relatives over to celebrate the occasion but the father is pretty preoccupied 

One of the darkest comedies I've watched. 
3. You should look at Bobcat Goldthwait's movies. Dark as HELL. World's Greatest Dad and Sleeping Dogs are just brutal. World's Greatest Dad stars Robin Williams of all fucking people!!
  - Love bobcats entire career, my favorite is God Bless America 
4. The Lobster. I went into it blind and it was disturbing. In retrospect, hilarious. It takes places in a world where if people aren't partnered up they get turned into an animal  of their choosing. Collin Ferrel just got divorced and has to get a new mate and he's at a place to meet one and ... yeah it's ... yeah

edit: i just remembered i went into it blind bc it was a free early screening and i remember the immediate  reaction after being like "wtf did i watch"
5. "Being John Malkovich" might fit the bill perfectly, but I don't know if it's black enough.
6. In Bruges, Seven Psycopaths, maybe 3 Billboards. All by the same director. All fab. 
7. I think Happiness is the MOST twisted,  you could laugh at Spun and Gummo , im not sure you can call them comedy.  Strangers with Candy is the must see of this genre.. movie and show 
8. Very Bad Things (1998)
9. Beau is Afraid

## 2. Did anyone ever hear of Dark City?

**Score:** 49
**Body:** It came out a year before The Matrix, explored similar reality-bending ideas, had a strikingly similar visual style, and even shared some of the same sets.

Yet one became a global phenomenon, while the other disappeared from most people’s radar.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fantastic movie. The directors cut is incredible.

But unfortunately, Dark City was presented more as a noir that dived into sci fi elements, meanwhile the Matrix was a hardline sci fi action movie with more impressive visuals.
  - Wanted to re visit for a while, thanks for the info of a directors cut.
2. It was too dark to appeal to a wide audience, the lead actors weren't famous, and there was little action. The bald figures, in particular, were really creepy. However, I like this movie because of that atmosphere. The view of the dock where Jennifer Connelly is standing is the best. It was excellent thanks to Kiefer Sutherland's performance. 
  - Jennifer Connelly stood at the end of a pier, with the camera shooting from behind, for dreamlike sequences in Dark City, Requiem for a Dream, and the House of Sand and Fog, which all released very close to one another. I used to think I remembered the films incorrectly, but it really *was* just super similar.
3. "Misssssttaa Muhhhhdockkkk"
4. Of course. It is popular amongst the movie nerds on Reddit. There is an argument that it is better than The Matrix but was far more concerned with the ideas than the special effects and thus did worse with the masses.
5. No one ever heard of it
6. Saw it in theaters. Amazing movie.
7. Ome of my Fav movies and one of my first DVDs back in the day.

Sewell and Jenn Connelly are amazing !

Sutherland was ok ....I guess

## 3. Boys weekend with my 14 y/o - what should we watch

**Score:** 30
**Body:** Looking for action, maybe sci-fi to watch with my son. He loved the matrix, lord of the rings, he’s so-so on Star Wars, and he’s not a big fan of Star Trek.

What do people think?

He wanted to continue watching the expanse- we’re starting season 3

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Fifth Element
2. Edge of Tomorrow
3. RAIDERS OF THE LOST ARK
4. Terminator or Back to The Future (bonus you get to watch T2 and BTTF 2+3 later on)
5. Independence Day

5th Element 

Galaxy Quest
6. Big trouble in little chins
  - Should be China but that typo is too funny to change 😂
7. It's the perfect time to introduce him to James Bond. 
8. Pacific Rim
9. Aliens, Predator, War of the Worlds, The Abyss, The Thing, Creepshow, Poltergiest, Outlander,

## 4. Movies with absurd/comedic concepts that evolve into something deep and heartwarming

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Hello Redditors, I'm looking for movie recommendations that will scratch a pretty specific itch of mine.

&#x200B;

Like the title suggests - I'm a sucker for movies (preferrably comedy ones, but I'm open to other genres) that start out with an absurd or funny concept, and uses that concept to discuss difficult topics.

&#x200B;

I know my description is a bit of a mess, so let me give a few examples:

&#x200B;

\- Bo Burnham's "Inside" - a story about a comedian who tries to perform without an audience inside of a single room, which transitions into discussions about the state of the world, and reflections about oneself.

&#x200B;

\- Samuel Abraham's "Lady" - a mockument about a woman who literally starts going invisible, which turns into a story about love, loss, and hope.

&#x200B;

\- "Little Miss Sunshine" - I love how a journey towards a seemingly innocent goal connects the family and gives insight into the relationships between them.

&#x200B;

Looking forward to your suggestions!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I <3 huckabees
2. The Full Monty - a group of unemployed steel mill workers form a striptease act.
3. Lars and the Real Girl, it tackled some real issues in an emotionally intelligent way while still being a lot of fun.
4. The Birdcage - a farce about a gay couple (Robin Williams and Nathan Lane) who have to play it straight when they meet their son's religious conservative in-laws (Gene Hackman and Dianne Wiest). And it's pride month so this is the perfect time to watch it
  - This is a perfect movie. Not just for this prompt but in general
  - The original *La Cage Aux Folles* is fantastic.  Give that one a try too.
5. Swiss Army Man. Totally absurd but definitely funny and a little heartwarming. I actually cried at the end in a bitter sweet way.
6. Everything everywhere all at once - laundromat owning couple need to pay their taxes becomes a story of a inter dimensional battle bought about by generational trauma 
7. Superbad
8. Brian and Charles - An eccentric loner creates a robot out of a washing machine but it’s really more a story of self-discovery. 

Where the Heart is - A knocked-up teen secretly lives in a Walmart after being abandoned by her boyfriend but then meets people who help her make a new life. Highly recommend this one. 

## 5. Just watched The Apartment (1960)

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I just watched this 1960 film, The Apartment, and now I am looking for something (film-wise) that touches me, and perhaps even heals me towards the end, and something that has a constant feeling of nostalgia and melancholy in it. 

The genre does not matter, and I am open to both colour and B&W films. 

While we can think like film students, focus on the hermeneutics and end up with an analysis paralysis, I think the film itself presented a very simple, relatable yet a very touching story. The beauty of it is that even if your own life story isn't congruent in any way, you can still put yourself in Baxter's or Fran's or even each of their shoes (without much effort) and be carried along by the narrative. That is precisely what I am looking for. :))

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I recently watched that as well! What a wonderful movie! It lead me to watch more of Jacks movies. Some Like it Hot for instance. 
  - I just watched this one recently and it’s so much more nuanced than I thought it was going to be. Nice job recommending this one it’s definitely got a similar energy..but wait I guess that’s just Jack? Haha!
2. Paper Moon
3. Marty (1955)
4. I love this movie. 

Have you seen Saturday Night Fever? 

hit me like The Apartment in some ways, way darker and deeper than I expected.

Also recommend the criminally underrated Men Don't Leave
5. Barefoot in the park was a fun little 1960s movie. I also like Peter Sellers movies.
6. Roman Holiday might be more of the bittersweet or sad but it’s also a fairy tale of a story and you feel good about things as well as wistful?
7. Breakfast at Tiffany's. It's funny, happy and then you find out her backstory... so many emotions. 
8. The Apartment's got that magic where it doesn't need to be your story to wreck you, and Ikiru might be the closest thing I've found to that same feeling, just quieter and more bittersweet.
9. The Way We Were definitely has the melancholy feeling

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Florence Pugh's career trajectory has been remarkable. She has consistently delivered good performances in both solo-led films and ensemble casts. What are your thoughts on Florence Pugh, and what's your favorite performance of hers?

**Score:** 630

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My intro to her was Midsommar and I've loved her since.
  - That was one hell of a movie. I had no idea going in and saw my wife watching it, so I sat down and was just mesmerized. 
2. She’s one of the most talented actors working right now. 
3. I have not seen her in all of those movies, but holy hell if she wasn't consistently one of the highlights in the movies I did see her in. Really good actress, imho, and seems to be good at selecting the roles she goes for.
4. Fighting with my family is such underrated movie
5. Stellar in Don't worry darling but I hated what they did in Oppenheimer. I find all Nolan's films Male heavy and the writing for women is shit.
  - Yeah, it was completely gratuitous 
6. Title drummer girl series also a great performance 
7. I feel like I instantly fell in love with her, respectfully…and it’s crazy to realize Fighting With My Family was prob the first one I saw. I forgot she was even in it. The Wonder was probably the next one, excellent as always. I’m still holding out on Midsommar 😅 I theoretically like psychological thrillers, but it’s hard for me to actually commit myself to watching one.

I haven’t seen all, but she was so captivating in Little Women. Loved that performance.
8. I thought she did a lot of heavy lifting in Thunderbolts.  She was a major part in making that movie great!

## 2. Excalibur is one of the coolest, most epic medieval fantasy films ever

**Score:** 514
**Body:** Real talk this movie holds up after all these years!

Visually stunning, epic scenery, and such a cool take on the Arthurian legend. The score gives me goosebumps to this day.

And I love how it leans into the dark, bizarre aspects of the Arthurian lore.

One of the best medieval fantasy films ever made!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You think you’re the king of England because some watery tart threw a sword at you?
  - “Now, once more, I must ride with my knights to defend what was, and the dream of what could be.”

I love this movie 💯
2. ![gif](giphy|E8wiDoHgZeGG0S1IEd)  
God I love this movie 
  - Listen. Strange women lying in ponds distributing swords is no basis for a system of government.
3. BEHOLD! EXCALIBUR! 

I love to think the director was sitting behind the camera constantly asking for more yelling.
  - https://i.redd.it/cwplae6uob8h1.gif
4. I’m old AF. Saw this multiple times at the theater when it came out. Big screen watches of this were awesome.
5. I'm convinced that this movie broke King Arthur movies. There hasn't been one anywhere near as good as this one and every attempt failed mostly because it always gets compared to this one. It's become the gold standard of Arthur movies and is probably also the reason why we don't see many attempts at it. We've gotten quite a few Robin Hood movies, but very few Arthur ones. 
6. The shot of Arthur and his Knights riding through the cherry blossoms falling around them while O Fortuna blasts through the soundtrack is so fucking epic and spectacular it gives me chills. The 4K Blu-ray from Arrow Video is an absolute treat if you love this movie. 
7. O fortuna playing during that ride through the cherry blossoms is epic 

## 3. My Mount Rushmore of movie scenes that absolutely tore me to freaking shreds, what are yours?

**Score:** 435
**Body:** Terms of Endearment 

I am Legend

Interstellar

Drag Me To Hell

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|qTOYz5dwQAPOU)  
Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan. You know the scene. 
2. For Gen X, this hit twice as hard when River died a few years later. 'I never had any friends later on like the ones I had when I was twelve. Jesus, does anyone?' “Stand by Me” (1986)

![gif](giphy|Z1f3wJ74gCrgA)
3. https://preview.redd.it/0x6w9maw498h1.jpeg?width=588&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=18f8f31848b99196f1aebccb66ad23a45c936266

  
End of Big Fish. It’s just so bittersweet and sweet and bitter and wonderful. 
4. “Brooks was here”
5. 1) fried green tomatoes, when Ruth goes while Idgy is telling her the pond story
2) never let me go, at the end when shes staring into the field
3) dear Zachary, when the grandpa talks about what happened to the baby and what he could have done to change it
6. Little women with Winona Ryder and Claire Danes. 

"If God wants me with Him, there is none who will stop Him. I don't mind. I was never like the rest of you... making plans about the great things I'd do. I never saw myself as anything much. Not a great writer like you... I love being home. But I don't like being left behind. Now I am the one going ahead. I am not afraid. I can be brave like you."

The "I can be brave like you...." - SOBBED 😭
7. https://preview.redd.it/ko4ztthvk98h1.jpeg?width=447&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=336fc717c190b5708d228e8e65daa06b7567384b

  
Jojo Rabbit is one of mine.
8. ![gif](giphy|12ncq33I7ZidwI)  
No matter how many times I watch up, I’ll always be blubbering after that opening montage. 
9. ![gif](giphy|3BZZb7JnaRArW2qBYz)

The other 3 would be:

1. Simba trying to reawaken Mufasa after he died in Lion King
2. My Girl (we all know which scene)
3. "Brooks was here"
10. ![gif](giphy|Vd2vQveLG1Ey5rbF39)

## 4. Who the hell dreams of working in a coal mine (queen of coal 2025)

**Score:** 378

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "the children yearn for the mines"

I wonder if it's intended to appeal to Minecrafters.
2. I've got the black lung pop!
  - "I'm not an ambiturner"
    - Damn it Derrick, you've been down there for one day, talk to me in 30 years!

  - https://preview.redd.it/cmzk6r2t398h1.jpeg?width=921&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=7b09cc4e26b07bac11b11790f73a350f4716ea5e
3. A trans woman coal miner? Talk about a four quadrant hit!
4. "I don't want to sell anything, buy anything, or process anything as a career. I don't want to sell anything bought or processed, or buy anything sold or processed, or process anything sold, bought, or processed, or repair anything sold, bought, or processed. You know, as a career, I don't want to do that."
5. This feels like coal mine propaganda 
  - I was watching Dopesick, a drama series about the opioid epidemic starring Michael Keaton, one of the main characters is a lesbian who works in the mines and won't stop despite losing her girlfriend and being in back pain.

What is going on in the mines? 🫪

## 5. Who's your favorite supporting character who's incredibly sweet? My favorite is Ursula from the Raimi Spider-Man universe.

**Score:** 328

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ruby, in Cold Mountain. 

Rene and Nicole were so great to see together.

https://preview.redd.it/6k4be149na8h1.jpeg?width=3840&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=b9be317b4b1d8ddbba2a62133959171cbf243958

But she's hot!

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Good film recommendations set in the Middle East

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Recently been thinking about Incendies and how unique it came off and was to me. So I was wondering if there are other films of similar quality of there exploring middle eastern people and culture. With there struggles and humanity as a central theme and idea. Want to see more representation and expand my film viewing list. Open to all and any recommendations. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Anything made by Abbas Kiarostami is fantastic in my opinion. Not only set in but made by an Iranian Filmmaker. Taste of Cherry and Close-Up are his most highly regarded films but I'm incredibly drawn to the Koker Trilogy. 

Everything I've seen from him really just captures life and humanity. 
  - Where Is The Friend's House? was really charming.
  - Taste of Cherry took me 3 watches to get past the first 30 minutes but when I finally did get through it, wow. One of the most important film experiences of my life. One of the only movies I’ve ever given a 10/10 from just my first watch 
2. The Salesman (2016) and A Separation (2011) by Farhadi are both incredible (and more accessible than Kiarostami if that matters).

It Was Just an Accident (2025) by Panahi is also excellent.
  - It Was Just An Accident is so excellent. Must watch. Prob my fave movie from last year.
3. Check out Kaouther Ben Hania films, shes a fantastic director from Tunisia. I love all her films, but if you want it set in the MENA region, check out “Beauty and the Dogs” or “Four Daughters”
4. Is there a specific region in the Middle East you are interested in learning about? It’s a large region and the cultures are distinct despite how Westerners tend to talk about our communities. 
5. Check out Kiarostami, he does great work centered around Iran. The movie Joyland is in Pakistan, which is kind of in between Middle East and Asia but it is very good and is focused a lot on Islam and is quite unique 
6. Amin Matalqa wrote and directed a movie called Captain Abu Raed. It tells the story of a Jordanian man who finds a pilot's hat at his job and uses it as a pretext to encourage children living in his neighborhood to look beyond their poverty by telling them stories about their heritage and the world beyond Aman. It is a deeply human film: 

https://tubitv.com/movies/593787/captain-abu-raed

Eran Riklis made a movie called Lemon tree that tells the story of an encounter between a Palestinian family and an Israeli politician who gradually takes over their lemon grove after he moves into a house next to theirs'. It understood everything that is happening in the Levant, now:

https://www.justwatch.com/us/movie/lemon-tree

Youssef Chahine made a movie called Cairo Station about a man who sells newspapers in a train station in Cairo who falls in love with a beautiful woman who sells drinks on a train. i have never been so surprised by the track a film takes than this one:

https://www.justwatch.com/us/movie/cairo-station

Naji Abu Nowar made a movie called Theeb that tells the story of a child who is swept up in a revenge conflict in Jordan during the first world war - its a brutal coming of age movie about the destruction of war:

https://www.justwatch.com/us/movie/theeb

Majid Majidi made a film called the Color of Paradise that tells the story of a intelligent and intuitively spiritual child who returns home from a special school for the blind for summer vacation. His return disrupts his family's life and causes enlightenment and shame:

https://www.dailymotion.com/video/x9t6pym
7. The Night of Counting the Years (1969)

Cairo Station (1958)

The Return of the Prodigal Son (1976)

Adieu Bonaparte (1985)

Paradise Now (2005)

Mayor (2020)

No Other Land (2025)

Waltz With Bashir (2008)

Lemon Tree (2008)

Farha (2021)

Persepolis (2007)

A Separation (2011)

Osama (2003)

## 2. Seeing Jaws in a theatre for the fist time.

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I'm very familiar with Spielberg's Jaws (1975) having seen it several times on tv. I consider it a master work of its genre (whatever that genre is?) So when I noticed a local cinema was showing a one off matinee I booked a ticket. It was a very sunny Friday afternoon and four people, including me, had turned up for the showing. Here are my takeaways from seeing Jaws on the big screen for the first time (things that were new to my experience of the film or that I hadn't fully appreciated before). This is going to be a bit of a flow of consciousness. 

The biggest revelation was the many town scenes in the first half. I hadn't fully appreciated how masterfully constructed they are. Often extremely busy and chaotic, Spielberg exploits the full width of the frame to include fore, mid, and background, building layers of action. And somehow the careful choreography always leads the eye and the ear to the important information. It's technically brilliant, and at the same time has a very natural flow to it. It powerfully communicates the character of the town - an expert piece of world building. Knowing the story well (and so unable to experience the intense feelings of anxiety Spielberg provokes), these town scenes were the most engaging aspect of the film for me. 

The ferry scene is a lesson in story telling. No joke - in the theatre this was my favourite scene in the film. It's when Brody hops on a ferry to travel across the bay and we meet the mayor and his fellow suits for the first time. For starters the scene is in one take - I'm not sure how long, possibly 2 minutes? The camera doesn't pan, it simply moves backwards once, and then again. Brody, the mayor and the two other men  begin the scene filling the left half of the frame. As the camera moves the actors step towards it, but one stays behind each time so that by the second move only Brody and the mayor remain in the foreground. With each movement the actors end up closer to the camera and further into the centre of the frame. As the dialogue gets more serious, more coercive, more ominous, the blocking becomes closer, more intimate, more intense. All the while, as the movement of the scene remains linear, the sky and shore revolve around them - it's a straight line within a circle. On top of all this, the actors are required to time the scene to exactly coincide wth the departure of the ferry and its arrival on the other pier. They nail it (how many takes I wonder?). It's just a thrilling set piece, and an example of what an extraordinary energy and life Spielberg can bring to an otherwise perfunctory bit of story telling. 

Robert Shaw is awesome. The cast of Jaws are all great, but in the theatre I saw Shaw's performance as Quint in a new light. He's magnetic, charismatic, and absolutely nails the salty old sea dog bit while never letting it slip into parody. He makes total sense of an arc that requires him to do random illogical things like smash up the radio or start singing during a shark attack. Amazing job. 

The people who complain about the fake shark have a point. I'd always defended the shark in Jaws, claiming it was convincingly realistic throughout. In the theatre, not so much. Especially towards the end of the film, the shark grows increasingly rubber. In Spielberg's defence, by the time the shark goes full Jim Henson we're already so locked into the story our disbelief could be suspended to almost any ridiculous degree. I was sitting close to a woman who was clearly seeing the film for the first time, and believe me, the rubber shark did not detract from her engagement with it. 

TLDR: Saw Jaws in a theatre. The 'town' scenes are amazing. The ferry scene is masterful and a highlight of the film. Robert Shaw is awesome. The shark is not very convincing towards the end.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The shark effect I assure you in its time was freaking *terrifying*.  (Yes, I saw the film way, *waaaaay* back in the Stone Age when it was originally released... *cough-old-fart-cough*).  Back then, unlike now, there was no instant access to real shark footage.  For many of us, this was the very first time we saw a shark presented in such a way and... it really worked.

What I've always been fascinated about regarding this movie is the way it tightens the suspense slowly but surely.

We have 3 leads: The veteran shark hunter (Robert Shaw) whose *whole life* seems to be about capturing sharks.  We, as the audience, feel confident he will surely capture the beast.  We have the book-smart shark expert (Richard Dreyfuss).  If Robert Shaw's Quint doesn't capture the beast, surely Dreyfuss' Hooper will figure out a way, using the latest technology, to capture and kill it.

And then there's Roy Scheider's Sheriff Brody.  Presented in the story as a literal fish out of water (ha!), he is uncomfortable in his job here while determined to do what's right.  By the end of the story, he states, as if we didn't know, how he used to hate the water...!  If there's a character who we think has the least experience and chance of getting the beast, it's him.

So what the film does so freaking wonderfully is strip us first of the veteran shark hunter.  Mentally, he's defeated when he tries to run back to shore, fleeing the shark while blowing the Orca's motor.  A little bit later, he would be defeated a second time and gone.  In between, Hooper tries his skills and is also defeated (and, for a little while, we fear he's dead as well).

Suddenly, the two people audiences felt would surely defeat the beast are gone and we're left with... the city guy.  The one who doesn't know a damn thing about sharks.  Who doesn't like the water.  Who has to read up on sharks in his dining room to figure out what they're about.

It's a *great* bit of subtle storytelling which raises the tension so wonderfully for audiences as we enter the movie's climactic third act!
  - I have a whole private theory about Jaws I think about in the shower. The three men in the boat each stand for differing belief systems. There is the man of faith (Quint), the man of science (Hooper), and the pragmatic man of the people (Brody). The story of the second half if the film is about how these three respond to an existential threat. It's interesting to me the way their allegiances to each other shift throughout the story. Quint, the man of faith, begins aggressively opposed to Hooper, the man of science. He sides with the pragmatist, Brody, imagining that it's with him common ground can be found. But over their journey, this dynamic shifts. Quint bonds with Hooper, who, after all, has a belief system of his own, and then it's Brody who is the odd one out. Turns out faith and science have more in common than they thought; more with each other than either with the non-believing, practical, pragmatic everyman. But it's the everyman who ultimately saves the world. The man of faith is destroyed by his own fatalism, and the man of science, while allowed to survive, proves entirely ineffectual. the message is that man should come first, because man is the force that can save us. God and science are welcome on the boat, but man should be the captain.
  - > Back then, unlike now, there was no instant access to real shark footage. For many of us, this was the very first time we saw a shark presented in such a way and... it really worked.

Never even considered this, thank you.
2. I watched this both on the anniversary release and as a young child of 7 on the initial release (the manageress of the local cinema was my grandmothers drinking buddy so we were allowed to watch the films from the disused balcony).

On first viewing as a child I did not have a bath for two weeks just in case....

What struck me on the rewatch on the big screen as well as all the other points mentioned was the shots when they are out on the boat.

It doesn't translate to the TV - but on the big screen the isolation and smallness of the boat and the three of them stuck out on the chase (which soon turns round the roles of predator and prey) really hits home.

In some shots the boat looks absolutely tiny.
3. I often feel that Carl Gottlieb doesn't get enough credit for the incredible adaptation he did of Benchleys book with the screenplay. I personally think Benchleys book is terrible for a number of reasons. But Carl's screenplay was masterful in what it leaves out, changes, distills. The combination of that writing with Spielbergs direction was truly beautiful. 
4. There are so many touches of artistry in this movie that I certainly wasn't paying any mind to when I saw it the first time in the theater as a kid in 1975.  

One great one that I didn't appreciate until a decades later rewatch was when the shark attacks in the harbor and bites the guy's leg off and you see the leg falling to the bottom. Then there's a shot of the Brody kid getting dragged out of the water with the camera following.  The camera stops as the kid is dragged past and you see that he has both his legs.
  - I thought about exactly this when I saw the film today! At that moment I thought - Spielberg knows the audience so well, he knows that as the boy is dragged from the water it will be in our minds 'is his body all there?' and so he makes that this little reveal. Absolute master.
5. I felt the same way watching it in IMAX earlier this year. The small town scenes were awesome, my partner and i talked about them and the ensuing hysteria a lot. It's a great American movie for sure

## 3. Learning to listen in Steven Spielberg's Disclosure Day (contains spoilers)

**Score:** 4
**Body:** **We Are Not Alone** by Aaron Lindquist



It’s been a while since I’ve fully embraced a film directed by Steven Spielberg. There are pieces of his recent films I love and others that haven’t reached me. I’ve watched *Disclosure Day t*wice now and see it as the return of an old friend.

	I was awed, not by grandiose visual effects or camera movement (Spielberg remains the undeniable master of “the oner”), but by the restraint used to depict a civilization-shifting event. The representational objects used by characters are almost Jungian in their symbolism. Remote viewing. Invisibility. Innate understanding of mathematics. Empathy. It’s striking how the characteristics of fairytales are used to tell the story. Two characters, boy and girl, follow self-conscious animals into a glowing house. The allusion to Hansel & Gretel is unmistakable. The mechanism for recreating their repressed memory is a reconstruction of a childhood home inside a warehouse.

	The clouds still look like ominous, late 18th century etchings with light pouring out of their black center, as if subconscious winds carried them from *Close Encounters of the Third Kind* to now. The alien machinery is less mythical and more realistic. In almost forty years we’ve culturally settled on a form and shape it has. 

	The story is bookended with a point of view shot. At the opening we, the viewer, are inside a wrestling ring, physically assaulted by another wrestler. The imagery is not subtle. We are being told to pay attention. The journey we’re on will have life or death consequences. We are also like the wrestler dragged around the ring, each day presented with an influx of negative news. It’s an onslaught so overwhelming that we attempt to not feel it. We numb ourselves and we tune out the people around us. Outside, control is maintained. We stop listening and let others maintain power through secrecy.

	Daniel Kellner (Josh O’Connor) is on the run after stealing seventy-nine years worth of data that proves the existence of extraterrestrial contact. Margaret Fairchild (Emily Blunt) is about to leave for work when the appearance of a red bird activates something inside her. She speaks an alien language during her weather report at KCXE in Kansas City, Missouri and collapses. After escaping black-suited agents at the hospital, Hugo Wakefield (Colman Domingo) calls Margaret to confirm she is part of a bigger plan. Noah Scanlon (Colin Firth) uses an alien device to find Daniel. He’s successful in remote viewing his girlfriend, Jane Blankenship (Eve Hewson). He insinuates himself in her thoughts and asks her questions that allow him to find their location. The story is a chase from start to finish.

	There are subtleties of disconnection throughout. From the boyfriend who doesn’t listen, to the girlfriend who never mentioned her spiritual past. Characters talk past each other rather than to each other until the stakes become clear. This isn’t a kids movie. Perhaps Daniel and Margaret could be seen as a grownup version of Elliott from *E.T.: The Extraterrestrial*. Unlike Elliott, they don’t know of their past encounter with extraterrestrials. Like Elliott, their role is that of an apostle meant to spread the great news. 

	Whereas *CE3K* was bounded on all sides by skepticism, *Disclosure Day* is populated with true believers who disagree who needs to know what the truth is. One of the central questions asked is whether acknowledging alien life discredits religion or if we are placing limits on a higher power by believing we are the only beings in creation. 

	Daniel carries a backpack filled with a cache of external hard drives. Each contain archives and records that prove the existence of extraterrestrial contact. Their rectangular shape reminded me of tablets engraved with the Ten Commandments. Like them, they contain revelations for the human race. 

	At the midpoint Hugo says, “We believe the believers and then we starve the rest of the population from believing them.” Noah’s response is that Hugo, “Got out of program,” and could no longer be trusted. He tells Noah that he got, “out of program,” when Noah lost his wife. He closed himself off and perpetuated a culture of secrets. Hugo explains to Noah what the extra-terrestrials have taught him: no civilization without empathy has survived. He says he once thought the way Noah did, but saw his error when he learned to listen. 

	Near the finale Jane asks Sister Maura (Elizabeth Marvel) if she believes the disclosure of extraterrestrial life will cause people to lose their faith. She responds, “I don't think you stopped believing in God. You stopped believing in people.” In the final act our protagonists return to KCXE in Kansas City. When disclosure happens it’s in the form of a news broadcast presented by Margaret, but it is Courtney Grace (portraying an NBC news anchor) who provides emotional connection as we look on seventy-nine years of UFO and alien footage that has been suppressed. I wasn’t alone in my tears.

	Pauline Kael highlighted the innocence of *Close Encounters of the Third Kind* in her 1977 The New Yorker review “The Greening of the Solar System”. While I still believe Spielberg has a childlike sense of wonder in his best films, he has reached a maturity here that offers wisdom. Spielberg, the man, appears to have changed. In interviews and marketing videos for the film, he seems explicit in practicing compassion. He’s gotten closer to his fans. He’s joined at the hip with his cast. He’s even talked about the meanings of his other films, which he declined to do before. He’s revealed that Roy Neary (in *CE3K*) will eventually return home and that Elliott never sees E.T. again, but he dreams about him. He’s shown a camaraderie that was less recognizable in the past. It’s remarkable that Spielberg has almost reached his eighties and his film feels like the vision of someone half his age. He continues to charm us with his wit and sense of boundless possibilities.

	As I watched *Disclosure Day* I thought of how human connection is the thru line of his films. It is empathy that results in the freedom of Africans sold into slavery in *Amistad*. It is empathy that frees David in *A.I.: Artificial Intelligence* when a crowd of luddites relate to an android’s humanity by yelling, “He’s just a boy.” Elliott and E.T. experience complete empathy, their emotions synchronized to each other, freeing them of the fear they are different from each other. Spielberg continues to see his aliens as benevolent, however they are frustrated with us. At the climax, the book-end point of view reasserts itself, tracking into a monitor while Margaret delivers an aging extra-terrestrial’s advice to humanity. Her last line is “Listen” because what could be more empathetic than to listen to each other? Our hopes and dreams as well as our grievances. We have tuned each other out. We want to be heard, we don’t want to listen. We want our desires met, we don’t want to consider their impact. Empathy has taken a vacation. When extraterrestrials offer their message to humanity we are on the brink of World War III. Spielberg is asking us, through his metaphor of extraterrestrial disclosure, to imagine a world in which we actually listened to each other and cared about the outcome. 

	We aren’t sent home with happy thoughts, unlike *CE3K* or *E.T.,* but we aren’t meant to. This is what elevates *Disclosure Day* into more than entertainment. We could call the conclusion anathema. Or we can take the hint to look at each other as the theater lights fade up. We are human. We share each other’s outcome. In that sense, we are not alone.

[You may also read this review on my Substack.](https://open.substack.com/pub/betterthanorwell/p/we-are-not-alone?r=42zgen&utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=web)

## 4. The lack of agency in Disclosure Day (2026)

**Score:** 1
**Body:** I recently watched Disclosure Day, felt like half-realized potential. The film seems to primarily concerned about the Truth and its suppression/revelation. So, alien conspiracy theories neatly tie into the theme and can simultaneously be used as a vehicle to deliver some action set-pieces and trademark Spielberg awe moments.

As the credits started rolling, I felt a little underwhelmed. I think its the lack of agency in the story's core. And I feel like the movie was hinting at it but, never established it well enough. I see Daniel and Margaret as rough stand-ins for logic and emotion. Daniel is gifted with extraordinary knowledge of math and is currently trying to reveal 'that' to everyone. His girlfriend, a novitiate who left the church, seems to be the counterpart to that drive, written to show Daniel that the sole pursuit and revelation of Truth isn't as good as it seems to be. You don't get to shove the truth down people's throats because, truth is only generative when people willingly pursue and receive it.

On the other hand, Margaret is gifted with absolute empathy. Powerful enough for her to lose her self-boundary by merely looking at people. While empathy is argued, and agreeably, to be probably the biggest strength for a living organism, the complete lack of choice for others to be seen or not to be seen complicates things. Empathy only makes sense when "I" can sufficiently differentiate myself from "You", without that boundary we are all worse off, I need to survive first to be able to help you survive. Nothing noble in both of us dying together. And, I think Margaret's boyfriend was written to show this need for boundaries, and by extension respect for the agency of the other. Either I missed it, or they completely scrapped this arc from the script.

I must say that, neither truth, nor care are asked to be ignored. Daniel's girlfriend struggles with the truth but, her faith only renews and strengthens from the struggle. So, capacity growing struggle is shown to be a good thing. Complete avoidance of pain (essentially ceasing to see any distinction between me and the world) is fundamentally capacity destroying, the capacity to experience anything. Scanlon and Wakefield act as stand-ins for something akin to the good and bad kinds of wisdom. They try to suppress/mediate the hard truth so that people's capacity for experience diminishes/grows.

I think the final scene is evidence for my interpretation, the alien whispers something to Daniel who then whispers it to Margaret. Showing that Truth needs to be experienced and delivered with empathy. One thing, that would have significantly improved the script (in my humble opinion) is the inclusion of agency, respect for choice.

Margaret could learn to respect boundaries and only "enter" people if they are willing to receive her. They clearly setup the negative version of it with Scanlon diving into Jamie to kill/pursue Daniel. She could have said that those who are willing to know the truth should stay and those who are not are free to turn the TV off. That would have conveyed the message that truth needs to received for it to be useful, not forced, because the former works for the other first and the latter works for you first.

The hansel and Gretel/(child abuse??) analogy used to characterize abduction definitely isn't a good thing, and both of these characters have been selected from youth to essentially participate in a predetermined ritual. I think that the underlying spiritual narrative of souls/ufology (knowing a little bit of the conspiracy lore) would dictate that Margaret and Daniel have chosen this life path for themselves on some intuitive, collective unconscious level, to assist in this step of human evolution. They're like Dave in 2001 or Richard Dreyfus in close encounters where the experience of otherworldy truth/trauma has left them immune to the fear aspect of it and willing to just lean in.
  - Would agree if they had at least a small indication that the children chose to receive their powers. Doesn't even need to be typical choice, even an intuitive grasp serves the purpose.
3. I wish that Daniel’s specific enhanced potential had been illustrated better in the film, on par with how we see how Margaret stretches into using her suddenly enhanced natural talents with empathy. 

I get it that it’s explained that Daniel has had his encounter with a cardinal years before but other than seeing him writing solved equations like it s taking dictation i don’t remember seeing him in action otherwise. It’s talked about a lot but not really shown.

## 5. E.T. Der Außerirdische ist Jesus – klingt witzig, ist aber nachvollziehbar

**Score:** 0
**Body:** &#x200B;

Kürzlich über eine interessante Interpretation von E.T. gestolpert, die ich vorher nie so bewusst gesehen hatte:

&#x200B;

E.T. kommt aus dem Himmel, vollbringt Heilungen und Wunder, schließt Freundschaft mit Menschen (Kindern im Film), wird von staatlichen Behörden verfolgt, stirbt scheinbar, kehrt ins Leben zurück und fährt am Ende wieder in den Himmel auf.

Klingt verdächtig bekannt.

&#x200B;

Natürlich ist E.T. kein christlicher Film im engeren Sinn. Aber viele Elemente erinnern an klassische Jesus-Erzählungen. Besonders die Auferstehungsszene und der Abschied am Ende wirken fast wie eine moderne Version religiöser Motive.

&#x200B;

Im Artikel werden die Parallelen genauer aufgeschlüsselt:

https://impuls.news/kultur/e-t-film-jesus-bibel-2/⁠

&#x200B;

Was meint ihr?

War Spielberg bewusst von christlicher Symbolik beeinflusst oder sieht man hier einfach Muster, die in vielen Geschichten vorkommen? Weil Spielberg hat das anscheinend verneint, also dass er das nicht bewusst so eingesetzt hat. Was man glauben kann oder auch nicht.
